coredata.dc:title: Sugar-metabolism-triggered pathogenic bacteria identification based on pH-sensitive fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Minyang
Gao, Xia
Tao, Zhanhui
Wang, Xinke
Lin, Xiaodong
Wang, Shuo
Liu, Yaqing
coredata.dc:description: Rapid distinguishing of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) with high sensitivity is critical for human health. Herein, a smart sugar-metabolism-triggered E. coli and S. aureus identification strategy is established on the basis of pH-sensitive fluorescent carbon dots (p-CDs). The fluorescence of the prepared p-CDs depends linearly on the solution pH in the acidic range from 4.0 to 7.0. When p-CDs are introduced into aqueous solutions that contain bacteria and sugar molecules, such as glucose or lactose, the fluorescence readout is reduced due to the acidic substances that are produced in the glucometabolic process of bacteria toward sugar molecules, which can decrease the pH of the sensing system. More importantly, E. coli and S. aureus differ in terms of their sugar metabolic capability on lactose and thus can lead to distinguishable fluorescence signals, which are used to further distinguish E. coli from S. aureus. The detection limits (LODs) for E. coli and S. aureus are 21 CFU mL-1 and 33 CFU mL-1, respectively, in the presence of glucose and 762 CFU mL-1 for E. coli in the presence of lactose. This p-CD-based fluorescence approach is simple, sensitive and free of any modification steps; thus, it has a much broader field of potential applications than other approaches.
coredata.dcterms:subject: fluorescence
p-CDs
sugar metabolism
bacteria identification

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation processes of p-CDs (A) and a TEM image (B, inset: HRTEM image), the particle size distribution (C), an FT-IR spectrum (D) and an XPS spectrum (E) of the as-prepared p-CDs.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of the p-CDs in DI water under various excitation wavelengths. (B) PL spectra and (C) the corresponding peak intensities of the p-CDs with pHs from 2.0 to 8.0. (D) The peak intensities of the p-CDs when the pH is switched between 8.5 and 3.0.

Anti-interference capabilities of the as-prepared p-CDs in aqueous solutions that contain (A) various metal cations (the concentrations of all ions are 0.5 mM) and (B) various concentrations of sodium chloride of up to 100 mM.
Normalized PL intensity of p-CDs with various types of bacteria in the absence or presence of glucose (A) or lactose (B).

Changes in pH in the presence of various concentrations of E. coli (A) and S. aureus (D) with 1 mM glucose. The normalized PL spectra of the p-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of E. coli (B) and S. aureus (E) with 1 mM glucose. The standard curves of E. coli (C) and S. aureus (F) detection in the range from 102 to 107 CFU mL-1.
(A) The normalized PL spectra of p-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of E. coli with 2 mM lactose. (B) The standard curves of E. coli detection in the range from 103 to 107 CFU mL-1.
Schematic illustration for distinguishing E. coli and S. aureus.
Truth table for distinguishing E. coli and S. aureus.
Bacteria determination in tap water samples based on our strategy.

head.ce:title: Sugar-metabolism-triggered pathogenic bacteria identification based on pH-sensitive fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin, 300457, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin, 300457, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
College of Food Science and Engineering
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid distinguishing of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) with high sensitivity is critical for human health. Herein, a smart sugar-metabolism-triggered E. coli and S. aureus identification strategy is established on the basis of pH-sensitive fluorescent carbon dots (p-CDs). The fluorescence of the prepared p-CDs depends linearly on the solution pH in the acidic range from 4.0 to 7.0. When p-CDs are introduced into aqueous solutions that contain bacteria and sugar molecules, such as glucose or lactose, the fluorescence readout is reduced due to the acidic substances that are produced in the glucometabolic process of bacteria toward sugar molecules, which can decrease the pH of the sensing system. More importantly, E. coli and S. aureus differ in terms of their sugar metabolic capability on lactose and thus can lead to distinguishable fluorescence signals, which are used to further distinguish E. coli from S. aureus. The detection limits (LODs) for E. coli and S. aureus are 21 CFU mL-1 and 33 CFU mL-1, respectively, in the presence of glucose and 762 CFU mL-1 for E. coli in the presence of lactose. This p-CD-based fluorescence approach is simple, sensitive and free of any modification steps; thus, it has a much broader field of potential applications than other approaches.

ce:section.ce:para: Diseases that are caused by pathogenic bacteria pose a substantial global threat to public life and health due to the increasing microbe contamination of food, drinking water and biomedical reagents [1–3]. Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) are two of the most common and dangerous clinical pathogens in our daily lives [4–6]. They can induce a series of life-threatening diseases in humans, such as diarrhea, anemia and even kidney failure [7–9]. Thus, it is urgently necessary to construct both sensitive and selective bacterial detection methods [10]. Although the classic culture- and polymerase-chain-reaction-based bacterial detection methods are reliable and accurate, the time-consuming operations, requirement for personnel training and false-positive/negative signals have limited their further development and application [11–14].

To overcome the drawbacks of traditional methods, substantial attention has been focused on developing strategies for rapid, simple and specific bacteria identification via the utilization of nanomaterials functionalized with recognition elements, such as antibodies and aptamers [15–19]. For example, Tan et al. developed antibody-conjugated carbon dots (CD)-encapsulated breakable organosilica nanocapsules for recognizing S. aureus [20]. Kearns et al. used magnetic nanoparticles to capture and isolate bacteria and specifically detected them via surface-enhanced Raman scattering nanoparticles that were functionalized with various types of antibodies [21]. Although the recognition performances of these functionalized nanomaterials toward specified bacteria can be improved, they still suffer from several issues: 1) Since each type of antibody or aptamer can only bind to one type of bacteria, multiple-bacteria detection can only be realized by using multiple modification strategies and by relying on multiple signal readouts, which is likely to be time-consuming and complicated. 2) Methods that utilize these nanomaterials demonstrate poor repeatability and stability due to differences among batches of antibodies or aptamers and to environmental factors [22]. Hence, there remains substantial room for the development of more simple and effective sensing systems for multiple-bacteria identification.

For the first time, herein, we established a simple and smart bacterial sugar-metabolism-triggered approach for identifying E. coli and S. aureus simultaneously on the basis of pH-sensitive fluorescent carbon dots (p-CDs). The p-CDs are prepared via a simple one-step hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and dopamine (DA), which are sensitive to solution pH values that are in the range from 4.0 to 7.0. When the p-CDs are introduced into aqueous solutions that contain pathogenic bacteria and sugar molecules such as glucose or lactose, the fluorescent readout changes linearly in response to the pH variation that is caused by the produced acidic substances in the glucometabolic process of bacteria toward lactose or glucose [23–25]. According to the difference in terms of sugar metabolic ability between E. coli and S. aureus toward lactose [26], E. coli and S. aureus can be distinguished from each other. Consequently, by coupling the p-CDs and sugar-metabolism ability of bacteria, the constructed p-CD-based fluorescent sensing system exhibits a surprising sensitivity, which is used to simultaneously distinguish E. coli and S. aureus. This fluorescent strategy is cost-effective and free of any chemical modification; hence, it partially addresses the issues that are discussed above.

o-Phenylenediamine (oPD) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd (Shanghai). Sodium chloride, copper nitrate trihydrate, potassium chloride, magnesium chloride hexahydrate, glucose and lactose were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai). Mercury standard solution was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai). Dopamine hydrochloride, calcium chloride, ferrous chloride tetrahydrate, ferric chloride hexahydrate, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, manganese chloride, europium chloride hexahydrate, terbium nitrate pentahydrate, nickel acetate tetrahydrate, lead acetate trihydrate and silver nitrate were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were conducted using a PHI-5000 Versaprobe (PHI, Japan). A Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained on a Bruker Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a LUMINA fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo, USA). A Milli-Q water purification system (Tianjin On-well Scientific Corporation, Tianjin, China) was used to purify and obtain high-purity water during the experiment.

First, 530 mg of dopamine and 380 mg of oPD were dissolved in DI water. Then, the solution was transferred into a poly(p-phenol)-lined stainless-steel autoclave for solvothermal reaction. After heating at 200 ℃ for 8 h, the solution was cooled to room temperature and was filtered through a filtration membrane (0.22 μm) to remove large-particle residues. Subsequently, the products were centrifuged at 10, 000 rpm for 15 min. After repeating the centrifugation process three times, the suspension solution was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da for 24 h and stored at 4 ℃ for further experimentation.

E. coli, S. aureus, Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium), and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) were revived via streaking on a nutrient agar plate. Before further operation, the activated bacteria were cultured with a lysogeny broth (LB) medium at 37 ℃ in a gyratory shaker at 200 r/min for 8 h. Then, the cultures were diluted to 10, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106 and 107 CFU mL-1 and, subsequently, were incubated in physiological saline that contained 1 mM glucose or 2 mM lactose at 37 ℃ for 6 h. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to remove the bacteria. Later, p-CDs were added into the suspension solution, and the fluorescence intensity of the system was determined by a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

To investigate the feasibility of our strategy, bacteria identification in various cases was conducted in real samples of tap water (from Tianjin University of Science and Technology). Prior to use, the tap water was filtered with a microporous membrane with a pore diameter of 0.45 μm and diluted 200 times. Then, altered concentrations of S. aureus, E. coli, or mixtures of S. aureus and E. coli were incubated in tap water that contained glucose (1 mM) for 6 h or lactose (2 mM) for 8 h at 37 ℃. Finally, the tap water samples were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to remove the bacteria, and p-CDs were added into each suspension solution, after which their fluorescence intensities were recorded. To avoid contamination during the pretreatment process, all tap water that was used was sterilized by high pressure steam at 121 ℃ for 20 min.

The p-CDs were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal treatment of oPD and DA under 200 ℃ for 8 h (Fig. 1 A). Herein, oPD and DA were selected as the main raw materials for the synthesis of the p-CDs for two reasons: the molecular structures of oPD and DA can generate large conjugated sp2-domains [27–28] and DA can produce nitrogen-containing groups on the surfaces of CDs to modulate their function [28]. In our study, DA was initially chosen with the objective of producing alkaline nitrogen-containing groups on the surfaces of p-CDs to sense acidic pH. A transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1B) shows that the p-CDs are well-dispersed, and their diameters are distributed in the range from 1.5 to 4.0 nm, with an average diameter of 2.7 nm (Fig. 1C). A high-resolution (HR) TEM image (the inset in Fig. 1B) shows identical well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which correspond to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra are detected and used to explore the functional groups on the surfaces of the p-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1D, functional groups that contain N-H bonds are present on the p-CDs, which correspond to the bands at 1325 cm-1 and 3215 cm-1. Hydrophilic groups such as O-H (3344 cm-1), C = O (1560 cm-1) and C-O (1261 cm-1) are also observed, which explain the satisfactory dispersibility of the CDs in water. Moreover, stretching vibrations that originate from C-H (3037 cm-1), C = C (1557 cm-1), C-N (1502 cm-1) and C-N= (1455 cm-1) bonds demonstrate the presence of polyaromatic structures and nitrogen-doped structures in the p-CDs. Subsequently, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations are used to further analyze the surfaces of the p-CDs. The full spectra (Fig. 1E) show three typical peaks of C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (400 eV), and O 1s (531 eV). In the HR spectra of C 1s (see Fig. S1A in Supporting Information (SI)), three distinct peaks that correspond to sp2 carbons (C-C/C = C, 284.6 eV), sp3carbons (C–N/C-O, 285.6 eV) and carboxyl carbons (C=O, 289.4 eV) are observed. The N 1s band (Fig. S1B) is divided into three peaks at 399.3, 400.1, and 401.6 eV; hence, amino N, pyrrolic N and N-H are present. The O 1s band (Fig. S1C) contains two peaks at 531.3 eV and 533.1 eV, which are attributed to C=O and C-O/C-O-C, respectively. These data clearly demonstrate that the as-prepared p-CDs consist of π-conjugated domains in their carbon cores and amorphous regions on their surfaces, which is consistent with the FT-IR results.

The photoluminescence (PL) properties of p-CDs are further investigated for sensor construction in aqueous solution. Fig. 2 A presents PL emission spectra of p-CDs in aqueous solution under excitation with various excitation wavelengths that range from 360 nm to 500 nm. It can be clearly observed that the PL emission peaks of the p-CDs do not shift with the excitation wavelength, which might be attributed to their uniform surface states [29–30]. This excitation-independent PL behavior is advantageous for sensor design and fabrication. Here, 420 nm is set as the excitation wavelength throughout the experiment to realize a high sensitivity. Interestingly, it is found that the p-CDs are highly sensitive to the pH value of the aqueous solution. As demonstrated in Fig. 2B and C, the PL intensity of the p-CDs changes slightly in the pH range of 2.0-4.0, gradually enhances as the pH increases from 4.0 to 7.0, and, finally, reaches a plateau in the basic region. The fluorescence property of CDs is closely related to their surface state. Therefore, we speculated that the p-CDs may possess basic groups on their surface, which can maintain the stability of the p-CD fluorescence under basic conditions and can be neutralized under acidic conditions to quench the fluorescence of the p-CDs. Interestingly, the PL intensity of the as-prepared p-CDs depends linearly on the solution pH under acidic conditions from pH of 4.0 to 7.0. The linear regression equation can be expressed as FI = 7.87 × 103 pH - 2.93 × 104 with an R2 value of 0.9901. Consequently, the p-CDs can act as a pH indicator of the aqueous solution in that the fluorescence of the system is quenched in acidic media but restored in basic media. As illustrated in Fig. 2D, the pH-dependent behavior of the p-CDs exhibits satisfactory reversibility: the PL intensity becomes weak at pH 3.0 and is restored once the pH of the system is tuned back to 8.5. The slightly decreased PL intensity of the p-CDs after three cycles could be attributed to their change of surface state, was an inevitable transformation of the surface state may occur during the continuous treatment between acidic and basic media.

The anti-interference capability of the p-CDs is further explored, which is critical for the successful construction of a sensing system. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence intensity of the p-CDs does not change substantially either in the presence of various metal cations or with high concentrations of sodium chloride of up to 100 mM, thereby suggesting excellent anti-interference capability of the p-CD-based sensing system and demonstrating substantial potential for practical applications, such as pathogenic bacteria detection.

Bacteria can decompose sugars such as glucose and lactose to generate acidic products [31–32], which is expected to influence the fluorescent response of the p-CD-based sensing system. With E. coli, S. typhimurium, S. aureus and B. subtilis as models, here, the sugar metabolism capabilities of the four types of bacteria are explored. Prior to further investigation, control experiments are conducted to determine whether the sugar molecules affect the PL intensity of the p-CDs. It is found that neither glucose nor lactose, even at high concentrations of up to 10 mM, substantially influences the PL intensity of the p-CD sensing system (see Fig. S2 in SI). In the presence of glucose, E. coli and S. aureus can effectively reduce the solution pH and, thus, decrease the fluorescence response of the sensing system. In contrast, the coexistence of glucose with S. typhimurium or B. subtilis has a slight influence on the pH of the sensing system (Fig. S3A in SI) due to the weak glucose metabolism. As a result, E. coli or S. aureus can be distinguished from the others according to the fluorescence response of the sensing system (Fig. 4 A). Moreover, when the bacteria are transferred to a sensing system that contains lactose, only E. coli can be distinguished from the other bacteria with a decreased pH value (Fig. S3B in SI). Similarly, a substantial PL intensity is readily observed only under the co-existence of E. coli and lactose (Fig. 4B). This is because only E. coli can express beta-galactosidase, which hydrolyzes lactose into glucose and galactose [25,33–34], which can cause a pH decrease due to the further metabolism of glucose. In the absence of glucose or lactose (the columns in Fig. S3 without glucose or lactose), the solution pH remains almost stable in the presence of all types of bacteria, thereby suggesting that no acidic products that are generated in the bacterial growth process [35] under our conditions affect the fluorescence of p-CDs without sugar molecules (the columns in Fig. 4 without glucose or lactose).

The performance of this p-CD-based fluorescent sensor in quantitative bacteria detection was evaluated with E. coli and S. aureus as examples in the presence of glucose. Since the pH modulation is based on the sugar metabolism of bacteria, it is important to optimize the glucose concentration that is used here to increase the detection sensitivity. If the concentration of glucose is too low, the acidic substances that are produced will not be sufficient for changing the solution pH, which could affect the detection limit. However, too high of a glucose concentration is also undesirable due to the possibility of overuse. Considering the similar sugar-metabolism capabilities of E. coli and S. aureus, we optimize the concentration of glucose that is needed in the presence of E. coli (Fig. S4A in SI). The optimal concentration is determined to be 1 mM, at which the largest change in the PL intensity can be detected. The incubation time is also examined to ensure the full decomposition of glucose by bacteria. As demonstrated in Fig. S4B in SI, after an incubation time with E. coli of six hours at 37 ℃, the PL intensity of the p-CDs tends to be stable. As a result, six hours is chosen as the reaction time for bacteria detection.

Under the optimal conditions, the constructed fluorescent sensor is used to detect E. coli and S. aureus in the presence of glucose. As shown in Fig. 5 , the pH value of the sensing system decreases from near-neutral to 4.0 as the concentration of E. coli (Fig. 5A) or S. aureus (Fig. 5D) increases. This phenomenon supports the feasibility of our proposed strategy. Consequently, the PL intensity of the p-CDs decreases linearly with the increase of the concentration of E. coli (Fig. 5B) or S. aureus (Fig. 5E) from 102 to 107 CFU mL-1. The linear regression equations can be expressed as y = 1.31-0.16 Log C E. coli with R2 = 0.9989 for E. coli detection and y = 1.29-0.14 Log C S. aureus with R2 = 0.9597 for S. aureus detection. By calculating three times the ratio of signal to background, the LODs are identified as 21 CFU mL-1 for E. coli detection and 33 CFU mL-1 for S. aureus detection, which are more sensitive than in most previous reports (Tab. S1 in SI).

In the above experiments, we have implemented a feasible strategy for the individual detection of E. coli and S. aureus. Now, we investigate how to distinguish E. coli from S. aureus. Since lactose can be decomposed into acidic substances by E. coli but not by S. aureus, a sensing system that contains p-CDs and lactose is constructed. Identically, the concentration of lactose and the incubation time with E. coli are optimal at 2 mM and 8 h (see Fig. S4A and 4B in SI). In the presence of lactose, the PL intensity of the p-CDs decreases linearly with the increase of the concentration of E. coli from 103 to 107 CFU mL-1 (Fig. 6 ). The linear regression equation can be expressed as y = 1.14-0.07 Log C E. coli with R2 = 0.9810, and the LOD is calculated to be 762 CFU mL-1. The PL intensity changes by a smaller amount (Fig. 6A) than in the presence of glucose (Fig. 5B) for E. coli detection. As a result, E. coli detection is less sensitive in the presence of lactose. A possible reason is that the acidic substances are produced via a two-step procedure in the presence of lactose, during which lactose is decomposed initially into glucose and subsequently into lactates and acetates [36–37].

According to the results we have obtained, E. coli and S. aureus can be separately and simultaneously detected by coupling the p-CDs and bacteria-triggered sugar metabolism. All the scenarios can be distinguished via a two-step procedure in which the bacteria sample and p-CDs are incubated with lactose and glucose, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . Two sensing systems were prepared: a system that contains p-CDs and glucose, namely, p-CDs-G, and a system that contains p-CDs and lactose, namely, p-CDs-L. If PL intensities of the two sensing systems remain constant, then neither E. coli nor S. aureus is present in the sample. If the PL intensity of p-CDs-G is reduced while that of p-CDs-L remains stable, only S. aureus is present in the sample. Since E. coli can quench the fluorescence of p-CDs-L and p-CDs-G, one cannot directly determine whether there contains only E. coli or both S. aureus and E. coli if the fluorescent signals of the two sensing systems were quenched. In this case, one can draw conclusions on the basis of the calculated concentrations of E. coli. Here, we suppose that only E. coli is present in the sample. Then, the concentrations of E. coli can be calculated as CE1 and CE2 in the p-CDs-L and p-CDs-G sensing systems, respectively. If CE1 and CE2 are equal, we speculate that only E. coli is present in the sample. If CE1 is lower than CE2, then E. coli and S. aureus co-exist in the sample. According to the above discussion, a truth table is constructed to simplify the analysis process. Here, a substantial fluorescent signal difference between the two sensing systems is assigned the logic value of “1”. The logic value of “0” represents that no substantial signal difference is detected between the sensing systems. For distinguishing the bacteria, “1” and “0” correspond to the presence and absence, respectively, of bacteria in the sample. According to the Truth Table 1 , one can feasibly distinguish all the four cases: containing neither E. coli nor S. aureus, containing only E. coli, containing only S. aureus, and containing both E. coli and S. aureus.

To investigate the potential application of our strategy to real samples, bacteria detection with the four cases, namely, “i) None”, “ii) S. aureus”, “iii) E. coli” and “iv) E. coli and S. aureus”, is conducted by spiking tap water with bacteria. As shown in Fig. S5, the normalized fluorescence intensities of p-CDs-L and p-CDs-G are recorded in the four cases. The initial fluorescence intensity of the p-CDs was set as 1. For case i), the fluorescence intensities of the two channels remain stable, thereby suggesting that no bacteria are present in the sample; for case ii), fluorescence quenching is only observed in the channel of p-CDs-G, thereby suggesting that only S. aureus is present in the sample; and for cases iii) and iv), fluorescence quenching is observed in both channels. To distinguish between cases iii) and iv), we supposed that only E. coli is present in the sample, and CE1 and CE2 were calculated using the p-CDs-L channel and p-CDs-G channel, respectively. As shown in Fig. S5, the calculated value of CE1 (2.6 × 105 CFU mL-1) is almost equal to that of CE2 (2.5 × 105 CFU mL-1) in case iii), thereby suggesting that only E. coli is present in the sample. However, for case iv), the calculated value of CE1 (2.8 × 104 CFU mL-1) is much smaller than that of CE2 (1.7 × 106 CFU mL-1); thus, we speculate that both S. aureus and E. coli are present in the sample. Table 2 summarizes the bacteria concentrations in all four cases that are determined via our assay, which have been further compared to the added concentrations to calculate the recovery rates. The results suggest that satisfactory recovery rates of 83.3% to 126.7% are realized for bacteria detection in tap water, thereby demonstrating the substantial potential of this assay for applications in real samples. In this work, we have raised a new principle for bacteria detection based on the sugar-metabolism of pathogens. However, it is worth noting that this work has limitations for identifying bacteria species in real world samples, in which various kinds of unknown bacteria can exist. Therefore, we will make more efforts in the follow-up work to figure this out.

In summary, pH-dependent p-CDs have been successfully synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment method. The PL intensity of the p-CDs can be simply regulated by tuning the solution pH in the acidic range. Coupling with the sugar metabolism that is triggered by bacteria, a strategy for the sensitive detection of E. coli and S. aureus is developed based on the sensing system of p-CDs in the presence of glucose. By further considering the difference between their lactose metabolism capabilities, E. coli can be successfully distinguished from S. aureus. All four cases, namely, containing neither E. coli nor S. aureus, containing only E. coli, containing only S. aureus, and containing both E. coli and S. aureus, can be conveniently distinguished according to the constructed truth table. The developed sensing system is free of any modification procedures and exhibits a wide linear range for E. coli and S. aureus detection with a satisfactory sensitivity, thereby providing a new avenue for the rapid detection of multiple bacteria.

Minyang Zhao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Xia Gao: Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Zhanhui Tao: Formal analysis, Resources. Xinke Wang: Software. Xiaodong Lin: Methodology. Shuo Wang: Supervision. Yaqing Liu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575138 and No. 21775108) and Construction of Platform for State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety (18PTSYJC00130).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128063.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Integrating recognition elements with nanomaterials for bacteria sensing
Chem. Soc. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Chen, S. M. Andler, J. M. Goddard, S. R. Nugen, V. M. Rotello, Integrating recognition elements with nanomaterials for bacteria sensing. Chem. Soc. Rev., 46 (2017), 1272-1283.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gram-positive antimicrobial activity of amino acid-based hydrogels
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Irwansyah, Y. Q. Li, W. Shi, D. Qi, W. R. Leow, M. B. Y. Tang, S. Li, X. Chen, Gram-positive antimicrobial activity of amino acid-based hydrogels. Adv. Mater., 27 (2015), 648-654.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibacterial activity of Graphene Oxide/g-C3N4 composite through photocatalytic disinfection under visible light
ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strong near-infrared absorbing and biocompatible CuS nanoparticles for rapid and efficient photothermal ablation of gram-positive and -negative bacteria
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces.

Han, Strong near-infrared absorbing and biocompatible CuS nanoparticles for rapid and efficient photothermal ablation of gram-positive and -negative bacteria. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces., 9 (2017), 36606−36614.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Méallet-Renault, Rapid and accurate detection of Escherichia coli growth by fluorescent pH-sensitive organic nanoparticles for high-throughput screening applications. Biosens. Bioelectron., 75 (2016), 320-327.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Minyang Zhao obtained his undergraduate degree at Bohai University (2017). He is a Ph. D student supervised by Prof. Yaqing Liu at State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Currently, his research is focused on developing fluorescent probe to detect pathogens in food and for biologic imaging.

Associate Prof. Xia Gao received her Ph.D. from the South China University of Technology in 2014. Before that, she was supported by a four-year joint training program at the Lab of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS). Later she studied as a postdoctoral fellow at Nankai University until June 2018. Now she is a research fellow of College of Food Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science & Technology. Her research involves in the design and fabrication of gold-related nanostructures for cell detection, in vivo analysis and Raman imaging. Until now she has published 5 SCI publications as the first author, including Chemical Reviews, Analytical Chemistry.

Prof. Zhanhui Tao obtained his BS at Chengdu University of Technology (1998). He has been a professor at Jilin Institute of Petrolchemical design and Research and currently works in Tianjin University of Science and Technology. His research work mainly focuses on organic synthesis and characterization.

Xinke Wang graduated from BoHai University in 2017. She is a master student supervised by Prof. Yaqing Liu at Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Currently, her research focuses on developing fluorescent probes to detect heavy metals, biotoxins, agricultural residues and illegal additives in food.

Xiondong Lin obtained his master degree at Tianjin University of Science and Technology (2017). He is a doctoral student supervised by Prof. Yaqing Liu at State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Currently, his research is focusing on construction of intelligent biosensor and bioimaging with novel nanomaterials.

Prof. Shuo Wang received his BS from Nankai University in 1991 and obtained his Ph. D from University of Sydney in 1999. He was a research fellow at University of Sydney and CSIRO Plant Industry from 2001 to 2003. He has been a professor at Tianjin University of Science and Technology since 2003 and currently works in Nankai University. His research is mainly focused on the fundamental theories and applied technology on rapid analysis of trace-level hazardous chemicals in food, and has made a number of pioneering achievements in related academic research frontiers, including the immunoassay for micromolecule, the enrichment and separation technology of trace-level contaminants in food, the synthesis and reaction mechanism of “biomimetic antibody” and the development on the immunoassay theory and technology of substitutable biological antibody. Prof. Wang has over 150 SCI publications. He is awarded Yangtze River Scholars Distinguished Professor, distinguished Young Scholar of the National Science Foundation of China.

Prof. Yaqing Liu received her BS from Chengdu University of Sciences and Technology in 1998 and obtained Ph. D from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2006. From 2007 to 2010, Dr. Liu worked as a postdoctoral fellow in Peter Grünberg Institute (PGI-8), Forschungszentrum Jülich, Germany. Then she joined Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry as associate professor in 2010 and became a full professor in 2012. Currently, Prof. Liu works in Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her research fields involve in synthesis of bio-nanomaterials, fabrication of molecular devices and molecular logic gates, which are further applied in bioanalytical and food security fields. The researches aim to develop novel approaches and technologies for intelligent rapid and accurate detection with high throughput, sensitivity and selectivity. Prof. Liu has over 50 publications, including ACS Nano, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., J. Am. Chem. Soc., Adv. Sci., Chem. Sci., NPG Asia. Mater., Nanoscale, Anal. Chem., Biosens. Bioelectron., Chem. Communi. etc.
